goal_17.config.title: The State of Development Data
goal_17.config.abstract: Without reliable data, the true extent of extreme poverty or gaps in children's health and learning outcomes remains hidden from the people tasked with fixing them. But as the World Bank's Statistical Performance Indicators (SPI) shows, low data availability is not inevitable. Some countries build strong, reliable data systems, even with limited resources.
goal_17.config.citation: Zander Prinsloo, Brian Stacy and Maarten Lambrechts. 2026. ”Can we measure progress?” In Atlas of Global Development 2026, edited by D Mahler, A F Pirlea, U Serajuddin, A Thudt, D Wadhwa and M Welch. Washington DC: World Bank. License: Creative Commons Attribution CC BY 3.0 IGO.
goal_17.config.inputs: Maria Eugenia Genoni and Alaka Holla
goal_17.config.homeFactoidText: of low- and middle-income countries, the most recent poverty survey is more than five years old
goal_17.config.homeTagline: For many countries, the data guiding budgets and regulations are years out of date, leaving millions of people invisible to policy makers.
goal_17.content.#1.text: Imagine you've just been appointed Minister of Finance. Tomorrow morning, you must decide where to build schools and clinics, how much to borrow, which programs to prioritize. Every decision will affect millions of lives. To govern well, you need answers to basic questions: How fast is the economy growing? How many children are healthy? How many young people are unemployed?
goal_17.content.#3.text: The answers to each of these questions lie in national accounts, population censuses, health surveys, household surveys, labor force surveys, and other types of data. Now imagine opening your briefing folders and discovering that much of the available data are years old.
goal_17.content.#4.title: In nearly
goal_17.content.#4.label: low- and middle-income countries, the most recent poverty survey is more than five years old.
goal_17.content.#5.text: Every day, governments make decisions that affect millions of lives, such as setting budgets and regulating the scope and quality of services. Yet for many countries, the data guiding those decisions are years out of date, leaving millions of people invisible to policy makers.
goal_17.content.#6.text: Take data on unemployment, for instance. For the average low- and middle-income country, the latest labor force survey is from 2019, the most recent poverty survey, from 2020, and the most recent health survey—measuring everything from child vaccinations to malnourishment and access to health care— is from 2015, more than 10 years ago.[footnote:  The latest labor force survey is calculated as the median year for all low- and middle-income countries. An analogous approach is used for poverty, health, and other surveys.] So, most of the surveys informing our current understanding of labor markets and public health are from before the COVID-19 pandemic.
goal_17.content.#7.text: In more than 30 low- and middle-income countries, the latest labor force, health, business, agriculture, and[emphasis:  ]poverty surveys [emphasis: are more than five years old]. In another 46 countries, four out five of these surveys are more than five years old. Together, this amounts to around 60 percent of all low- and middle-income countries. So, when these countries try to make data-informed decisions, they are charting their future using an outdated picture.
goal_17.content.#8.title: Many countries rely on survey data that are more than five years old
goal_17.content.#8.subtitle: Average age of latest surveys in low-and middle-income countries
goal_17.content.#8.keyFactLabel: of low- and middle-income countries, the most recent poverty survey that is more than five years old.
goal_17.content.#9.text: When survey data are missing, policy makers rely on models. But these, while useful, can miss the truth by a wide margin, as illustrated by Nigeria's poverty data.[footnote:  Poverty estimates are drawn from the World Bank's Poverty and Inequality Platform (PIP). The 2018 figure (34.2 percent) and the extrapolated 2022 figure (34.8 percent) are based on Nigeria's 2018 Living Standards Survey (LSS), with the extrapolation projecting poverty forward using national accounts (gross domestic product or GDP) data. The survey-based 2022 figure (41.8 percent) was incorporated in PIP's Spring 2025 update, following the release of Nigeria's 2022 LSS. The seven  percentage point gap between the extrapolated and survey-based 2022 estimates illustrates the limitations of nowcasting as a substitute for timely household survey data.] In Nigeria’s case, any programs targeting households by poverty status would have missed millions of households.
goal_17.content.#10.title: Nigeria’s rate of extreme poverty
goal_17.content.#10.subtitle: Survey vs extrapolation estimates
goal_17.content.#10.visdescription: Comparison of extrapolated versus actual survey-based poverty estimates for Nigeria (2018–22), showing how national accounts-based projections underestimated the true poverty rate revealed by the 2022 household survey.
goal_17.content.#10.scenes.#0.text: In 2018, survey estimates revealed that around 34 percent of Nigerians (71 million people) were living in extreme poverty.
goal_17.content.#10.scenes.#1.text: Without updated survey data, poverty monitoring between 2018 and 2022 relied on model-based projections, which estimated that the poverty rate had remained broadly stable, at 34.8 percent.
goal_17.content.#10.scenes.#2.text: The 2022 survey data revealed that the poverty rate was actually 41.8 percent, substantially higher than the projected 34.8 percent.
goal_17.content.#10.scenes.#3.text: The seven percentage point discrepancy corresponds roughly to [emphasis: an additional 17 million additional Nigerians living in extreme poverty], underscoring the limitations of modeling as a substitute for timely household survey data.
goal_17.content.#12.text: The timeliness of statistics is not the only issue. In many cases, the methods and data used to calculate statistics are outdated, producing misleading estimates. An example from Ghana illustrates this starkly. When it updated its national accounts framework in 2010, incorporating new industrial census data, value added tax records, and household surveys and modernizing the way it classified the services sector, reported GDP rose by more than 60 percent.[reference: devarajan][reference: jerven_duncan] Three-quarters of that increase came from the services sector alone, which had been systematically undercounted under the old framework. But many countries continue to use old methodologies, with 52 still using a 1993 manual to report GDP statistics today.
goal_17.content.#13.title: Changes to the methodology and data used to produce GDP led to a
goal_17.content.#13.label: increase in Ghana’s estimated GDP in 2010.
goal_17.content.#15.text: The absence of data also severely limits a country's ability to understand the needs of important populations, such as children, leaving policy makers unable to address the most pressing challenges they face.
goal_17.content.#16.title: The number of children living in a country with no recent internationally comparable learning assessment is
goal_17.content.#16.label: Representing roughly half of children in the age group
goal_17.content.#17.title: Key data are missing for millions of children
goal_17.content.#17.source: World Bank’s Learning Poverty and Country Learning Outcomes database (learning assessment); UNICEF/WHO/The World Bank: Joint child malnutrition estimates (JME) database (stunting); UNSD Population and Vital Statistics Report (birth registration)
goal_17.content.#17.note: Availability of data between 2019 and 2024 for the following indicators: births registered with a civil authority (% of children under five);
goal_17.content.#17.keyFactLabel: children live in a country with no recent internationally comparable learning assessment.
goal_17.content.#17.scenes.#0.text: Around [emphasis: 162 million children] under five are unaccounted for in birth registries, making up about 30 percent of all children globally.
goal_17.content.#17.scenes.#1.text: These gaps are concentrated in specific regions. In Sub-Saharan Africa, around half of all children under five were never registered at birth.
goal_17.content.#17.scenes.#2.text: Around [emphasis: 200 million children] live in countries with no recent data on child growth, which are needed to measure malnutrition. In East Asia & Pacific and Sub-Saharan Africa, stunting data—the primary measure of whether young children are adequately nourished—are missing for more than half of all children under five.
goal_17.content.#17.scenes.#3.text: Around [emphasis: 777 million children] aged 7 and 14—roughly half of all children that age—live in countries with no comparable learning assessment in the past five years. In East Asia & Pacific, South Asia, and Sub-Saharan Africa, there are no comparable data on whether more than half of these children can read or understand basic math at age-appropriate levels.
goal_17.content.#18.text: Without these data, it is impossible to know how many children live in a country and whether they are healthy and learning. And without such basic information, how can a government plan where to build schools and clinics, how many teachers and health care staff to hire, or what kind of programs are needed to address nutrition or skill deficits before they become permanent?[footnote:  Birth registration data measure the share of children under five whose births have been officially recorded with a civil authority. Stunting (low height-for-age) is the primary indicator of chronic undernutrition in children under five. Learning assessment figures refer to internationally comparable assessments conducted since 2019..]
goal_17.content.#20.text: The SPI measures statistical systems across five pillars using 51 indicators that cover everything from whether surveys are conducted to whether key data are produced and used, data are accessible online, and the legal foundations and financial resources for statistics are in place.[footnote: The five pillars are data use, services, products, sources, and infrastructure. For data to have an impact, they must first be used. The data use pillar also measures whether data meet the needs of key users of a statistical system, such as the Minister of Finance. Pillar 2 measures whether services, such as online accessibility, are available to connect users to data. The data products pillar measures whether the system produces key statistics, including those concerning the well-being of children, to meet the needs of users. Reflecting the challenges that many statistical systems encounter, the data sources pillar tracks the availability of foundational data sources, while the data infrastructure pillar assesses the hard and soft infrastructure, such as statistical legislation, financing, and the methodologies that underpin statistical production.] It includes an overall score that measures statistical performance on a scale from 0–100, where 100 represents the best possible statistical system.[reference: dpss]
goal_17.content.#21.title: Statistical performance varies widely across economies
goal_17.content.#21.subtitle: The SPI measures the strength of statistical systems
goal_17.content.#21.keyFactLabel: High-income countries score 34 points higher than low-income countries on the World Bank’s Statistical Performance Indicators index.
goal_17.content.#21.scenes.#0.text: Statistical performance varies widely across economies. Norway has an SPI score of around 94 points, close to the maximum of 100, while South Sudan scores just 27 points.
goal_17.content.#21.scenes.#1.text: The SPI allows you to examine the performance along five key dimensions: data use, data services, data products, data sources, and data infrastructure.
goal_17.content.#21.scenes.#2.text: High-income, OECD economies dominate the top tier of statistical systems, accounting for more than 90 percent of countries in the top quintile. The top performers include Scandinavian countries such as Norway (94.1) and Sweden (92.7). Performance at this level is strong and relatively even across all five pillars.
goal_17.content.#21.scenes.#3.text: In the next quintile (60th–80th percentile), the composition shifts: upper-middle-income economies make up over half, and lower-middle-income economies account for about 16 percent. This shows that strong statistical performance extends well beyond high-income economies.
goal_17.content.#21.scenes.#4.text: The middle quintile shows more unevenness across the five pillars; it is where the highest-performing low-income countries begin to appear. Burkina Faso, for example, stands out as a low-income country performing at levels comparable to many middle-income peers.
goal_17.content.#21.scenes.#5.text: In the third and fourth quintiles, weaknesses in data sources begin to emerge, with performance on Pillar 4 starting to lag noticeably behind the other pillars. This signals gaps in the foundational surveys, censuses, and administrative systems needed to produce reliable statistics.
goal_17.content.#21.scenes.#6.text: In the 5th quintile—the bottom 20 percent of economies—a striking pattern is the presence of many Small Island Developing States. These economies can face persistent capacity constraints, even at higher income levels. Examples include St. Kitts and Nevis (42.7), Nauru (47.9), The Bahamas (51.3), and Guyana (54.1). In the bottom quintile overall, Small Island Developing States and fragile and conflict-affected (FCV) countries together account for the vast majority of cases, underscoring that vulnerability and scale can be as important as income.
goal_17.content.#21.scenes.#7.text: About 60.5 percent of FCV countries fall in the lowest SPI quintile. Yet these are precisely the settings where timely, credible statistics are most essential for crisis response and effective policy design, making investment in statistical systems especially urgent.
goal_17.content.#21.scenes.#8.text: The gap is starkest for Pillar 4 (data sources). FCV countries tend to score extremely low on the ability to produce the building blocks of statistics—surveys, censuses, administrative data, geospatial data, and so on—even relative to other countries in the bottom quintile. Without these foundations, both measurement and policy targeting become much less reliable.
goal_17.content.#22.text: A country’s income tends to be correlated with the performance and maturity of its statistical system. But this relationship is not deterministic. Many countries perform at the same level as far richer countries, providing valuable insights for how to improve statistical systems in the places where it is most needed.
goal_17.content.#23.title: Income matters, but it is not destiny
goal_17.content.#23.textParagraph: Countries such as Mexico, Burkina Faso, Senegal, Uzbekistan, and the Philippines substantially overperform relative to what would be predicted from their GDP per capita, showing that institutions and sustained investments can build high-performing statistical systems at modest income levels. By contrast, Libya, Equatorial Guinea, Turkmenistan, St. Kitts and Nevis, and Andorra are notable underperformers, with SPI outcomes well below what their income levels would suggest, highlighting that resources alone do not guarantee statistical capacity.
goal_17.content.#24.text: Building capacity for mature statistical systems
goal_17.content.#25.text: The information available to guide policy is often incomplete, outdated, or uneven in quality. These constraints are not distributed randomly. They tend to be most binding in low-income and fragile and conflict-affected (FCV) settings, where the demands on the state are greatest and the costs of policy error, highest. It is precisely in such settings that rapid shifts in economic conditions, prices, displacement, and service delivery can quickly render old statistics misleading.
goal_17.content.#26.text: But the evidence also cautions against a deterministic view of capacity. Several countries, such as Mexico, Burkina Faso, Senegal, Uzbekistan, and the Philippines, achieve levels of statistical performance comparable to peers with far higher GDP per capita.
goal_17.content.#27.text: The implication is straightforward but important: while resources matter, income alone does not determine statistical effectiveness. Institutions, incentives, and sustained investments in statistical systems can address deficits.
goal_17.content.#28.text: For policy makers and development partners, this finding points to a need to organize improvements in statistical performance around strengthening data sources. This includes traditional instruments, such as household surveys and administrative systems, and complementary sources, such as geospatial and other high-frequency data. At the same time, modernizing methods for integrating, validating, and using these inputs is vital.
goal_17.content.#29.text: Emerging technologies, including artificial intelligence, offer genuine opportunities to reduce the costs of data collection and processing and expand data use by improving discoverability, timeliness, and dissemination. But realizing these gains will require deliberate investments in governance, skills, and quality assurance, as well as technology.
goal_17.content.#30.text: National statistical offices can rarely meet these demands in isolation. Building durable foundational and frontier statistical capacity will increasingly depend on international partnerships that support training, peer learning, and the adoption of best-practice standards. For a finance minister tasked with making decisions under uncertainty, such investments are not ancillary. Rather, they are part of the core infrastructure of effective economic management.
goal_17.references.devarajan: Devarajan, S. 2013. Africa’s Statistical Tragedy. Review of Income and Wealth 59: 9–15.
goal_17.references.jerven_duncan: Jerven, M and Duncan, M E. 2012. Revising GDP Estimates in Sub-Saharan Africa: Lessons from Ghana. The African Statistical Journal, 15(15):13–24.
goal_17.references.dpss: Dang, H A H, Pullinger, J, Serajuddin, U and Stacy, B. 2023. Statistical Performance Indicators and Index—A New Tool to Measure Country Statistical Capacity. Scientific Data 10(1): 146.
